panel.aurora.title: Aurora
panel.aurora.probability.tooltip: OVATION modelled chance of visible aurora overhead at this point
panel.aurora.kp.tooltip: Planetary geomagnetic activity index; higher values mean stronger disturbance and aurora reaching lower latitudes
panel.aurora.kp.band.quiet: Quiet geomagnetic field; faint aurora confined to the polar oval
panel.aurora.kp.band.active: Active field; aurora visible from high latitudes such as Iceland or northern Scandinavia
panel.aurora.kp.band.storm: Minor to strong storm (G1–G2); the oval expands to mid latitudes like southern Canada or the northern US
panel.aurora.kp.band.severe: Severe to extreme storm (G3–G5); aurora may reach low latitudes and can disrupt satellites, GPS and power grids
panel.sat.title: Satellites
sat.no_passes: No passes above the horizon today.
sat.col.name: Sat.
sat.locked_note: Position locked to nearest valid time (orbit data out of window).
ecliptic.label: Ecliptic
equator.label: Celestial Equator
panel.sun.title: Sun / Day-night
panel.sun.astro_twilight.tooltip: When the Sun's altitude is between −18° and −12°, as faint objects gradually emerge
panel.sun.nautical_twilight.tooltip: When the Sun's altitude is between −12° and −6°, when the horizon is faintly discernible
panel.sun.civil_twilight.tooltip: When the Sun's altitude is between −6° and 0°, when no artificial lighting is needed outdoors
panel.sun.sunrise_sunset.tooltip: When the Sun's altitude is −0.83°, with its disc tangent to the horizon after atmospheric refraction
panel.sun.transit_time.tooltip: Moment the Sun crosses the meridian at local solar noon, reaching its highest altitude of the day
panel.sun.transit_altitude.tooltip: Highest altitude the Sun reaches above the horizon today
panel.sun.golden_hour.tooltip: When the Sun's altitude is between −4° and +6°, bathing the light in a warm, soft gold
panel.sun.blue_hour.tooltip: When the Sun's altitude is between −6° and −4°, when the sky deepens to a saturated blue
panel.moon.title: Moon / Planets
panel.moon.transit.tooltip: When the Moon crosses the meridian at its highest altitude of the day
panel.galactic.title: Galactic core
panel.galactic.warning.no_astro_night: ⓘ Sun does not drop below −18° tonight, no astronomical night (polar day or low-latitude summer)
panel.galactic.warning.core_too_low: ⓘ Galactic core peaks too low (max {maxAlt}°), severe atmospheric extinction
panel.galactic.warning.moon_too_bright: ⓘ The Moon is {moonIllum}% lit tonight; moonlight strongly interferes
panel.galactic.warning.moon_overlap: ⓘ Core visibility overlaps with moonrise, no dark moonless window tonight
panel.galactic.transit_time.tooltip: When the galactic core reaches its highest altitude of the day
panel.galactic.transit_altitude.tooltip: Maximum altitude the galactic core can reach above the horizon today
panel.weather.title: Weather
panel.lp.title: Light pollution
panel.lp.index.tooltip: Ratio to natural sky brightness; higher values indicate worse light pollution
panel.lp.zone.0: Pristine night sky; zodiacal light visible, with the Milky Way and deep-sky objects fully structured
panel.lp.zone.1: Near-pristine sky; faint glow only near the horizon, with the Milky Way richly detailed
panel.lp.zone.2: Rural sky; faint glow low in a few directions, with deep-sky objects easily seen
panel.lp.zone.3: Outer-suburban sky; artificial glow nears natural brightness, with the Milky Way still visible
panel.lp.zone.4: Suburban sky; artificial glow is marked, with the Milky Way visible only overhead
panel.lp.zone.5: Inner-suburban sky; the sky glows greyish, with the Milky Way only faintly visible at the zenith
panel.lp.zone.6: City sky; the sky is pale grey and the Milky Way has essentially vanished
panel.lp.zone.7: Bright city sky; only a few stars and planets remain visible
panel.lp.unit_brightness.tooltip: Natural night-sky brightness at the zenith; higher values mean less light pollution
panel.lp.error.out_of_bounds: No light pollution data for this location
panel.lp.error.fetch_failed: Failed to fetch light pollution data
eclipse.lunar.range.p1: P1 Visibility Circle at Penumbra Begins
eclipse.lunar.range.u1: U1 Visibility Circle at Partial Begins
eclipse.lunar.range.u2: U2 Visibility Circle at Totality Begins
eclipse.lunar.range.u3: U3 Visibility Circle at Totality Ends
eclipse.lunar.range.u4: U4 Visibility Circle at Partial Ends
eclipse.lunar.range.p4: P4 Visibility Circle at Penumbra Ends
eclipse.list.title: Eclipse events
pev.list.title: Planets & Comets
pev.desc.new_moon: The Moon's ecliptic longitude coincides with the Sun's (0° apart), its dark side toward Earth, invisible all night.
pev.desc.first_quarter: The Moon's ecliptic longitude is 90° ahead of the Sun's, its western half sunlit, standing due south at sunset and setting near midnight, visible through the first half of the night.
pev.desc.full_moon: The Moon's ecliptic longitude is 180° from the Sun's, its sunlit face fully toward Earth, visible all night.
pev.desc.last_quarter: The Moon's ecliptic longitude is 270° from the Sun's, its eastern half sunlit, rising around midnight and visible through the second half of the night.
pev.desc.comet_perihelion: {body} reaches the point of its orbit closest to the Sun, {dist} AU away, where solar heating peaks and physical changes are most intense.
pev.desc.comet_perigee: {body} passes closest to Earth, {dist} AU away, at its brightest and moving fastest across the sky.
glossary.pev_moon_phase: The changing sunlit fraction of the Moon as the Sun and Moon shift in relative position; new, first quarter, full and last quarter mark its four defining moments
glossary.pev_perihelion: The point on an orbit nearest the Sun, where solar heating is strongest and a comet is often brightest and most active
glossary.pev_perigee: The moment a body passes closest to Earth, when its apparent brightness and motion across the sky are greatest
glossary.pev_comet_milestone: The moment a comet's brightness crosses a round apparent magnitude; 6 is roughly the naked-eye limit under a dark sky, 3 an easy naked-eye object, 0 a brilliant one
glossary.pev_opposition: A superior planet 180° from the Sun in ecliptic longitude
glossary.pev_conjunction: Two bodies sharing the same right ascension, appearing close together in the sky
glossary.pev_conj_inferior: An inferior planet at 0° ecliptic longitude from the Sun, between Earth and the Sun
glossary.pev_conj_superior: An inferior planet at 0° ecliptic longitude from the Sun, beyond the Sun from Earth
glossary.pev_quadrature: A superior planet 90° from the Sun in ecliptic longitude
glossary.pev_greatest_elong: The moment an inferior planet's apparent distance from the Sun reaches its extreme
glossary.pev_station: A planet's apparent motion reversing between prograde and retrograde
glossary.pev_separation: The apparent angular distance between two bodies in the sky
glossary.pev_elongation: A body's apparent angular distance from the Sun
glossary.pev_constellation: The constellation the body occupies at this moment
pev.desc.opposition: {body} stands opposite the Sun, closest to Earth and visible all night, making this the best time to observe it.
pev.desc.conjunction: {body} passes behind the Sun, rising and setting with it and remaining unobservable all day.
pev.desc.inferior_conjunction: {body} passes between Earth and the Sun, making it essentially unobservable.
pev.desc.superior_conjunction: {body} passes behind the Sun, rising and setting with it and remaining unobservable all day.
pev.desc.quadrature_east: {body} stands 90° from the Sun with its phase defect at maximum, appearing gibbous, reaching its highest point after sunset, the best time to observe it in the evening.
pev.desc.quadrature_west: {body} stands 90° from the Sun with its phase defect at maximum, appearing gibbous, reaching its highest point before sunrise, the best time to observe it at dawn.
pev.desc.greatest_elongation_east: {body} reaches its greatest angular distance from the Sun, appearing in the western sky after sunset, the best time to observe it.
pev.desc.greatest_elongation_west: {body} reaches its greatest angular distance from the Sun, appearing in the eastern sky before sunrise, the best time to observe it.
pev.desc.station_retrograde: {body}'s apparent motion against the stars reverses from prograde to retrograde, beginning a period of retrograde motion.
pev.desc.station_direct: {body}'s apparent motion against the stars reverses from retrograde back to prograde, ending its retrograde period.
time.range.limit: This site only carries astronomical data for 2000 to 2099.
panel.eclipse.title: Eclipses
panel.eclipse.next_solar_partial.tooltip: Next solar eclipse reaching at least partial phase here
panel.eclipse.next_solar_total.tooltip: Next solar eclipse reaching total or annular phase here
panel.eclipse.next_lunar_partial.tooltip: Next partial lunar eclipse visible here
panel.eclipse.next_lunar_total.tooltip: Next total lunar eclipse visible here
glossary.ecl_none_here: No eclipse of this kind is visible here before {year}
glossary.ecl_ecliptic: The track the centre of Earth's shadow traces: the ecliptic
glossary.ecl_moonpath: The track the Moon traces through Earth's shadow: the lunar path; the arrow marks its direction of motion
glossary.ecl_sunpath: The Sun's apparent path across the sky; the arrow marks its direction of motion
glossary.ecl_par: Duration of the partial phase of a lunar eclipse
glossary.ecl_c_greatest: Greatest eclipse, the instant of maximum coverage
glossary.ecl_c_p1_solar: First contact, the Moon's disc first touches the Sun and the partial phase begins
glossary.ecl_c_p2_solar: Second contact: the Moon fully covers the Sun and totality or annularity begins
glossary.ecl_c_p3_solar: Third contact: the Sun begins to reappear and totality or annularity ends
glossary.ecl_c_p4_solar: Last contact, the Moon's disc leaves the Sun and the eclipse ends
glossary.ecl_c_sunrise: The Sun rises already eclipsed, the first contact lay below the horizon before sunrise. Here sunrise is the instant the Sun's centre reaches 0° apparent altitude (refraction included), unlike an ordinary sunrise set at the upper limb (about −0.83°)
glossary.ecl_c_sunset: The Sun sets while still eclipsed, the fourth contact lies below the horizon and is not visible from here. Here sunset is the instant the Sun's centre drops to 0° apparent altitude (refraction included), unlike an ordinary sunset set at the upper limb (about −0.83°)
glossary.ecl_c_moonrise: The Moon rises already eclipsed, the earlier contacts lay below the horizon before moonrise
glossary.ecl_c_moonset: The Moon sets while still eclipsed, the later contacts lie below the horizon and are not visible from here
glossary.ecl_c_p1_lunar: The Moon enters Earth's penumbra, its disc dimming slightly
glossary.ecl_c_u1: The Moon's edge enters Earth's umbra and the partial phase begins
glossary.ecl_c_u2: The Moon is fully inside the umbra and totality begins
glossary.ecl_c_u3: Third contact: the Moon's edge leaves Earth's umbra and totality ends
glossary.ecl_c_u4: The Moon fully leaves the umbra and the partial phase ends
glossary.ecl_c_p4_lunar: The Moon leaves Earth's penumbra, its disc restored to full brightness
glossary.ecl_total: Duration of the total phase of a lunar eclipse
glossary.ecl_gamma: Least distance of the Moon's centre from the shadow axis in Earth radii, positive when north of the axis
glossary.ecl_umag: Umbral magnitude, the fraction of the Moon's diameter covered by the umbra at greatest eclipse
glossary.ecl_pmag: Penumbral magnitude, the fraction of the Moon's diameter covered by the penumbra at greatest eclipse
sidebar.locate.unsupported: Cannot locate: browser does not support it
share.button.title: Copy link
glossary.window_almanac: Continuous span after dark when a planet's altitude is ≥ 0°; the Moon's window also covers daytime
glossary.window_sat: Continuous span after dark when the satellite's altitude is still sunlit, so its glint is visible from the ground
glossary.window_galactic: Continuous span during astronomical night when the galactic core is above 10° and the Moon is below the horizon or less than 30% lit
glossary.magnitude: Apparent brightness of a body as seen by the observer; smaller is brighter, with the naked-eye limit about 6.5 under a natural night sky
glossary.absolute_magnitude: The apparent magnitude a body would show at 10 parsecs (about 32.6 light-years), reflecting its intrinsic luminosity
glossary.apparent_diameter: Apparent angular size of the body's visible disc
glossary.ra: Longitude in the celestial equatorial system, measured eastward from the vernal equinox; each hour spans 15°
glossary.dec: Latitude in the celestial equatorial system, 0° at the equator, positive north and negative south
glossary.altitude: Angle of the body above the horizon
glossary.peak_altitude: Highest altitude the body reaches within its observation window
glossary.azimuth: Angle of the body relative to due north
glossary.elevation: Highest elevation the satellite reaches on this pass
glossary.sat_pass: Pass interval while the satellite's altitude is ≥ 0°
glossary.distance: Straight-line distance from the body to the observer
glossary.substellar_point: Point where the line from the body through Earth's centre meets the ground
glossary.latitude: Angular distance of a surface point from the equator, positive north and negative south
glossary.longitude: Angular distance of a surface point from the prime meridian, positive east and negative west
glossary.visible_period: Time interval when the object is above the horizon and can be seen during the night
glossary.na_below_horizon: The body stays below the horizon all night, so there is no observation window
glossary.na_daylight_only: The body has set by the time the sky darkens, so there is no observation window
glossary.na_moon_always_up: The Moon stays above the horizon all night and is visible throughout
glossary.na_moon_always_down: The Moon stays below the horizon all day and never rises
glossary.na_sun_above: The Sun stays above this altitude all day and never descends to it
glossary.na_sun_below: The Sun stays below this altitude all day and never rises to it
glossary.cloud_cover: Fraction of the sky hidden by cloud; lower means clearer skies
glossary.seeing: Strength of atmospheric turbulence; lower means steadier star images
glossary.transparency: Clarity of the atmosphere; higher means sharper star images
glossary.astro_night: Period when the Sun's altitude is below −18°, when twilight has fully faded and deep-sky observing is possible
glossary.galactic_core: Direction toward the centre of the Milky Way in Sagittarius, its brightest and densest region
glossary.spectral_type: The classification of a star by its spectrum, mainly reflecting surface temperature (O blue to M red) with a luminosity class
glossary.color_index: Difference in a star's magnitude under blue (B) and yellow (V) filters; smaller means bluer and hotter
glossary.effective_temp: Blackbody temperature matching the body's total radiated power
glossary.ecliptic_lon: Longitude in the ecliptic coordinate system, measured eastward from the vernal equinox along the ecliptic
glossary.ecliptic_lat: Latitude in the ecliptic coordinate system, 0° on the ecliptic, positive north and negative south
glossary.phase_angle: Angle between the Sun and Earth as seen from the body, 0° at full phase and 180° at new phase
glossary.illumination: Visible fraction of the body's sunlit face, 100% at full phase
glossary.libration: Slight periodic rocking of the Moon's face relative to Earth
glossary.saros: Serial number of an eclipse within its saros series; successive eclipses are about 18 years 11 days apart
glossary.obscuration: Fraction of the Sun's area covered by the Moon
glossary.gamma: Least distance of the shadow axis from Earth's centre in Earth radii; smaller absolute values are more central
glossary.ecl_magnitude: Fraction of the body's diameter covered at greatest eclipse
glossary.duration_total: Length of time an observer experiences a total eclipse
glossary.duration_partial: Length of time an observer experiences a partial eclipse
glossary.duration_penumbral: Length of time the Moon lies within Earth's penumbra
glossary.contact_exterior: Instant the discs touch externally, marking the start or end of the partial phase
glossary.contact_interior: Instant the discs touch internally, marking the start or end of totality or annularity
glossary.greatest: Moment of maximum magnitude, when the discs' centres are closest
glossary.u1_first: Moment the Moon starts entering Earth's umbra and the partial lunar eclipse begins
glossary.u2_total_begin: Moment the Moon is fully within Earth's umbra and the total lunar eclipse begins
glossary.u3_total_end: Moment the Moon starts leaving Earth's umbra and totality ends
glossary.u4_last: Moment the Moon fully leaves Earth's umbra and the partial lunar eclipse ends
glossary.penumbral_contact: Moment the Moon touches the outer edge of Earth's penumbra, beginning or ending the penumbral eclipse
glossary.geo_distance: Distance from the body to Earth's centre
glossary.helio_distance: Distance from the body to the Sun's centre
glossary.parent_body: Comet or asteroid that sheds the meteoroids forming the shower
glossary.zhr: Zenithal hourly rate: the ceiling on meteors visible per hour under ideal clear skies with the radiant at the zenith
glossary.velocity: Speed of a meteoroid entering the atmosphere relative to Earth; faster yields brighter meteors
glossary.solar_longitude: Solar longitude, marking Earth's position along its orbit relative to a comet's stream of orbital debris
glossary.dso_size: Angular size the object presents to the observer
glossary.dso_type.GCl: Dense spherical cluster of hundreds of thousands of old stars bound by gravity
glossary.dso_type.OCl: Loose group of young stars born together from one nebula
glossary.dso_type.Neb: Interstellar cloud of gas and dust
glossary.dso_type.PN: Glowing shell of gas cast off by a low- to mid-mass star late in life
glossary.dso_type.SNR: Expanding cloud of debris left by a massive star's supernova
glossary.dso_type.G: Vast gravitationally bound system of stars, gas and dark matter
glossary.dso_type.Cl+N: Young star cluster still embedded in its parent nebula
glossary.dso_type.HII: Cloud of hydrogen gas ionised and lit by hot stars
glossary.dso_type.RfN: Dust cloud that shines only by reflecting light from nearby stars
glossary.dso_type.*Ass: Loosely scattered group of stars sharing a common origin
glossary.dso_type.Other: Deep-sky object not falling into the above categories
glossary.moonphase.new: Moon lies between Earth and Sun, its dark side toward Earth, nearly invisible
glossary.moonphase.waxing_crescent: After new moon the lit face grows into a hooked sliver, appearing in the western sky after sunset
glossary.moonphase.first_quarter: Moon 90° from the Sun in ecliptic longitude, western half lit, due south at sunset and setting at midnight
glossary.moonphase.waxing_gibbous: After first quarter the lit face is more than half and still brightening
glossary.moonphase.full: Moon opposite the Sun in ecliptic longitude, fully lit and visible all night
glossary.moonphase.waning_gibbous: After full the lit face is more than half but fading
glossary.moonphase.last_quarter: Moon 270° from the Sun in ecliptic longitude, eastern half lit, rising at midnight and due south at sunrise
glossary.moonphase.waning_crescent: After last quarter the lit face thins to a hooked sliver, appearing in the eastern sky before dawn
glossary.moon_age: Days since the last new moon, counting from 0; one cycle of phases takes about 29.5 days
glossary.catalog.hip: Hipparcos catalogue number; released in 1997 from the high-precision astrometry of the Hipparcos satellite, it lists about 120,000 stars
glossary.catalog.hd: Henry Draper catalogue number; compiled by Harvard College Observatory and released in 1918 to 1924, the first large catalogue of stellar spectra, listing over 200,000 stars
glossary.catalog.hr: Yale Bright Star Catalogue number; released in 1930 to 1991, it lists nearly 10,000 naked-eye stars
